np.float64(62.0)

Summary

This report reviews the application rounds of 2024 and 2025 as well as the ongoing 2026 application round of the LERU Law PhD Exchange Programme. With this review we want to clarify the scope and functioning of the exchange programme as well as the role and workload for the coordinators. We summarise where the applicants come from, their preferences, the outcomes of the allocation process (from initial allocation to finale outcome), compare this with the number of availabe positions, and finally analyse the relation between home universities and destinations.

Key findings

  • With regard to the applicants, we see that overall participation is uneven and highly variable. Less than one third of the participating institutions has 5 or more applicants (Figure 1, Table 1).
  • Preferences are likewise unevenly distributed, with some destination (mainly Cambridge, UCL, but also Edinburgh and Amsterdam) consistently attracting a disproportionate number of first-choice applications (Figure 2 and Table 2).
  • The initial allocation process (matching applications to destinations) is relatively effective. A large share of applications can be assigned to the first or second preference (80% in 2025), and this process can be completed within two weeks after the submission deadline (Figure 3 and Table 3).
  • Final outcomes are significantly lower than initial assignments, with a substantial number of applications that remain unresolved after allocation. This is due to delayed responses, unclear decisions, and long communication chains. In 2025, only 60% of the applications that were initially assigned to a destination were effectively accepted (Figure 4 and Table 4).
  • Overall, the number of applications that are effectively accepted is significantly lower than the number of available positions. While in 2025 we managed to assign applicants to 73% of the available positions, this substantially drops at the acceptance stage. For 2026, we have assgned 51% of the available positions. Here too, the result is uneven, with 4 destinations reaching 100%, and 12 reaching at least 50% (Table 5).

Improvements and current situation

After the 2025 application round, the high number of unresolved application was flagged as the main issue. As this was at least in part due to the process overlapping with the Summmer break, we moved the deadline forward to the 4 May 2026.

  • 4 May 2026: 51 were submitted (compared to 75 in 2024 and 80 in 2025).
  • 20 May 2026: 46 applications assigned to destinations and 5 remain provisionally unassigned.
  • 25 June 2026: 26 (51.0%) applications accepted.

In view of the current status, we expect that that we will be able to complete processing of applications before the Summer-break. Applications that are allocated, but for which no final or provisional decision is received, will be closed.

Overview of the time investment of coordinators

On a yearly basis, the estimated time-investment consists in:

  1. A fixed part to prepare the yearly application round. This includes collecting information from all the partners, updating the website, and distributing the call-materials to all partners. This is estimated at 2 working days.
  2. A variable part that depends on the number of applicants. This is estimated at 30 minutes per applicant, and includes:
    • Downloading and reorganising the application materials
    • Checking each application
    • Running and fine-tuning the distribution
    • Handing over the applications to the different partners (staff at participating faculties can consult the applications online, but they each receive an email with an update)
    • Processing acceptances and informing candidates
    • Ad hox inquiries, trouble shooting, and sending reminders to partners and candidates.
  3. A variable part for platform maintenance and improvements, extensive work on the website, and communication and reporting on the exchange scheme at LERU-meetings. This is estimated at 5 working days.

Based on the historical figure of 80 applicants, this leads to an overall estimate of 12 working days per year.

The initial time-investment (mainly between 2022 and 2024) for the development of the online application platform is not taken into account in this yearly overview. Time spent on the development and refinement of the application platform is estimated at 40 working days.

Overview of the data

  • the applications: how many applications are there and what are their home universities?
  • the preferences of the applicants: which destinations do they list as their first, second and third preference?
  • the results of each application round: how are candidates matched to their preferences, how many of them are accepted, and how does this compare to the number of positions that are available in theory?
  • the relations between home universities and destinations: where do candidates come from and where do they go?

A note about the data: the data used in this report are based on the information accessible to the coordinators. This included the information stored on the application platform as well as the Excel-sheets we use to keep track of all applications. These data may not reflect the reality in at least two ways. Acceptances that are communicated directly to the candidates, but not to the coordinators remain invisible. Conversely, visits that are cancelled after acceptance migth not always be registered either.

Applications

In 2024 we received 75 applications, in 2025 80, and in 2026 51. Application numbers per year and home university (Figure 1 and Table 1) reveal that participation is uneven (less than one third of the participating faculties has 5 or more applicants on a yearly basis) and highly variable in the sense that the number of applicants for each university varies over the years.

Preferences

As noted, the preferences of the applicants are unevenly distributed, with some destination (mainly Cambridge, UCL, but also Edinburgh and Amsterdam) consistently attracting a disproportionate number of first-choice applications. Figure 2 and Table 2 show the distribution of preferences for each year.

Assignments and acceptances

Assignments

We first examine at the assignments without taking into account whether candidates whose application is transferred to a destination is actually accepted. At the aggregated level (Figure 3), we see a high share of allocations to first and second preferences. Moreover, the number of not assigned candidates remains low. This suggests that, at least in theory, we succeed rather well in matching offer and demand.There are no indications that a further decrease of the number of not assigned candidates is desirable. Some applicants are interested in an alternative opportunity (a fourth preference), but this remains exceptional (claim based on communications with the candidates).

The unaggregated data in Table 3 (separating the applicants of each home university) also confirm that there are no substantial discrepancies across universities. Applicants from all home universities have a high chance of being assigned to their first or second preferences.

Acceptances

When we examine what happens after the applications were distributed, we see that in practice we cannot make most of the theoretically available positions. Even though we can assign a large share of applicants to a potential destination, these assignments do not always lead to an effective acceptance. This happens in two situations. Some applicants cannot be accepted at the faculty or department they were assigned to. This is most often due to the fact that either the timing does not work out, because of a lack of fit, or because a suitable academic host is not available. In such cases applicants are either rejected or (if still possible)redirected to another potential destination. In many cases, however, no final decision reaches the coordinators.

The results for 2024 and 2025 in Table 4 and Figure 4 are final, whereas the results for 2026 capture the situation on 25 June 2026. We first look at the relation between preferences (first, second, third) and final results. Next, we refine this to reveal differences between destinations.

How do assignments and acceptances then compare to the number of positions that are available at each participating faculty or department? Table 5 shows the number of applicants assigned to each destination, the resulting acceptances, and the percentage of the available positions that were effectively used. The total (theoretical) number of available positions was 101 (resp. 91 for 2026).

  • In 2024 62% of the available positions were assigned to a candidate, and 55% were effectively filled.
  • In 2025 73% of the available positions were assigned, and 50% were effectively filled.
  • For 2026, currently 51% of the available positions have been assigned. Final results cannot yet be provided.

Home universities and destinations

Figure 5, Figure 6, Figure 7 below depict for each year where candidates come from and where they go. The figures are organised such as to place on top the home universities (y-axis) with many applicants, and the destinations (x-axis) with many incoming visitors on the left. This ordering is based on the aggregated results of the past 3 years. The left-hand figures show the results based on assignments, the right-hand figures are based on acceptances. A light-blue cell denotes the assignment/acceptance of one candidate, a dark blue cell of two candidates.
